title: Document Processing Capstone
concept: Building a full ingestion pipeline: parse, extract metadata, handle tables, then chunk; 'Next up: Vector Database Foundations.'.

exercises.prompt: Why do we use layout-aware parsing instead of basic text extraction for multi-column PDFs?

options.text: It translates the document into multiple languages automatically.
It prevents text from different columns from being incorrectly merged together.
It compresses the file size to make downloads faster.

exercises.explain: Layout-aware parsing respects the physical columns and reading order of a page instead of reading naive horizontal lines across the entire page.

cards.title: From Raw Files to Clean Data
cards.body: We have learned how to extract text from layout-heavy PDFs, but how do we tie parsing, table handling, metadata tagging, and chunking together into one automated assembly line? Let's build a complete ingestion pipeline.

cards.title: Document Ingestion Pipeline
cards.body: A document processing pipeline is a sequence of steps that takes raw, messy files (like PDFs, Word docs, or scans) and transforms them into clean, structured, and metadata-rich text chunks ready for an LLM to read and query.

cards.title: The Processing Pipeline Factory
cards.body: Think of a document ingestion pipeline like a recycling plant sorting center. First, you shred the incoming waste (parsing), scan for valuable plastics and glass to label them (metadata extraction), separate special items like aluminum cans (tables), and finally pack them into neat, identical bundles for shipping (chunking).

cards.title: A Pipeline in Action
cards.body: In a legal AI application, the pipeline parses a 50-page contract, extracts the 'Execution Date' as metadata, converts a complex payment schedule table into a clean Markdown table, and splits the document into 500-token chunks while appending the metadata to every single chunk.

cards.title: The 'Dump and Run' Trap
cards.body: Many developers think you can just dump parsed text directly into an LLM database. In reality, without separating tables, extracting metadata, and chunking carefully, your LLM will retrieve irrelevant paragraphs and hallucinate wrong answers.

cards.title: Chaos vs. Structure
cards.body: Compare a chaotic raw text dump to a structured ingestion pipeline output.

left.text: A giant wall of text with table rows mixed into paragraphs, losing all context and document structure.

right.text: Clean text chunks, tables formatted in Markdown, and tags (metadata) showing the source document and page.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need a dedicated step for handling tables in a document processing pipeline?

options.text: Because tables are always written in a foreign language.
Because naive text extraction merges rows and columns into unreadable sentences.
Because LLMs cannot read numbers unless they are converted to words.
Because tables automatically delete metadata if not processed first.

exercises.explain: Tables contain structured, relational data that gets scrambled when read as normal paragraphs. Treating them separately (e.g., converting to Markdown or JSON) preserves their meaning.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Chunking should be done before layout-aware parsing and table extraction to save time.
exercises.explain: Chunking is the final step where the cleaned, structured, and metadata-tagged text is split into smaller, manageable pieces that fit within the LLM's context window.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: Adding contextual labels to your text chunks is called extracting ________.
exercises.sentence: To keep track of where information came from, our pipeline attaches metadata to each text chunk.
exercises.explain: Adding metadata like source, page number, and category to chunks prevents the LLM from losing context when it retrieves isolated text fragments.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI-generated code/description for a pipeline step:

options.text: Step 1: Read PDF with layout-aware parser. Step 2: Detect tables and convert to Markdown. Step 3: Attach source metadata. Step 4: Chunk text into 300-word blocks.
First, use layout-parsing to extract clean text. Then, extract metadata like author and date. Next, isolate tables, and finally split everything into overlapping chunks.

def run_pipeline(doc):
    # Initialize pipeline process
    print("Starting process...")
    doc = doc.process_now()
    doc = doc.clean_all_text()
    return doc # Pipeline completed successfully

exercises.explain: Option C is generated by an AI assistant trying to sound like a program, but it uses highly repetitive, generic filler code that doesn't implement actual processing logic. Options A and B represent human pseudo-code describing the logical pipeline steps.

exercises.prompt: You are building a production-grade ingestion pipeline for a library of financial reports containing text, columns, and financial balance sheets. Which sequence represents the most robust pipeline to prepare this data for an LLM system?

options.text: Naive plain-text extraction -> Tokenization -> Flat database insertion
Layout-aware parsing -> Table isolation & Markdown formatting -> Metadata extraction -> Semantic chunking
OCR scanning -> Stopword removal -> Full-text database indexing
Document translation -> Summary generation -> Random sentence splitting

exercises.explain: A complete ingestion pipeline first parses layout-aware text, isolates complex table elements to process them with specialized models (or Markdown conversion), attaches metadata (like document type, date, or source), and then chunks the text logically so the vector database receives clean, contextual fragments. Next up: Vector Database Foundations.
